---
title: "12.4 Reworking a project in Rust"
---

## Explanation

After completing a Julia or Python scientific-computing project, redo a small,
well-defined part of it in Rust with help from an AI agent. Do not begin by
translating the whole project. Choose a function, kernel, or command whose
inputs, outputs, tests, and benchmark case are already clear.

This is where TDD pays off. If the original project was developed as small
tested functions, each tested behavior becomes a natural migration unit. Port
one function or component, run matching Rust tests, read the Rust code, and only
then move to the next part. Without tests, you are translating a script and
guessing whether it still means the same scientific computation.

Do not treat generated Rust as a black box. After translating one part, inspect
the code visually. When you find a line, type, function signature, ownership
rule, or borrow that you do not understand, ask the AI agent to explain that
exact point until you can explain the code yourself.

The workflow is:

1. Start from the existing specification and saved-data convention.
2. Choose one tested function or component as the first Rust target.
3. Ask the AI agent to summarize the Rust data structures and ownership design.
4. Ask the agent to create or update a Cargo project.
5. Port one small component first.
6. Write unit tests that match the Julia or Python behavior.
7. Read the generated Rust code with the agent and resolve unclear parts.
8. Add a benchmark for a fixed problem size.
9. Compare output, runtime, and memory behavior with the original implementation.
10. Move to the next tested function or component only after the first one is clear.

For the Ising project, a suitable first target might be the deterministic energy
and local energy-change functions, then the update loop. For the tight-binding
DOS project, a suitable first target might be the dispersion calculation or
k-mesh energy generation.

Benchmarking is part of the exercise, but it comes after correctness. Use the
same input size, seed policy where relevant, and output convention. If the Rust
version is faster but produces different data or omits metadata, it is not a
successful scientific implementation.

## Things to look up

- Cargo project
- `cargo test`
- `cargo run`
- `cargo bench`
- Criterion benchmark
- Rust ownership
- Rust slice
- Mutable borrow
- Function signature
- Unit test

## Exercise

Choose one completed Julia or Python project and ask an AI agent to implement
one small Rust component. The prompt should include:

```text
Use the existing project specification.
Implement only the following component in Rust: ...
Keep the same inputs, outputs, conventions, and validation checks.
Add unit tests and one benchmark case.
Before editing, summarize the Rust data structures and ownership design.
After editing, explain every function signature and every mutable borrow.
Do not proceed to another component until tests pass and I can explain this one.
```

After the code is generated, compare it with the Julia or Python version.
Explain:

- what data structure represents the main array,
- which functions mutate data,
- which functions only read data,
- how ownership or borrowing appears in the function signatures,
- whether the tests check the same behavior as the original implementation.
- one line or signature that you did not understand at first, and what the AI
  agent's explanation changed in your understanding.

## Notes for the exercise

- Do not ask the agent to translate the entire project in one step.
- TDD makes migration incremental because tested functions and components give
  natural Rust porting units.
- Do not accept generated Rust only because it compiles.
- Do not benchmark before the tests and validation checks are meaningful.
- Keep one benchmark case fixed so that later changes are comparable.
- Inspect the generated code visually. Ask the AI agent to explain unfamiliar
  syntax, but verify the explanation against official documentation when needed.
